name: Armenia
tagline: From the kingdom of Urartu and the world's first Christian state through Ani and the genocide to a republic reborn in 1991.
summary: Armenia occupies one of the oldest continuously inhabited corners of the world, the Armenian highlands south of the Caucasus, where the Iron Age kingdom of Urartu and later an Armenian kingdom that briefly stretched from the Caspian to the Mediterranean under Tigranes the Great took shape. Around 301 CE, under King Tiridates III and Gregory the Illuminator, Armenia became the first state to adopt Christianity as its religion, and a century later Mesrop Mashtots created the Armenian alphabet — twin pillars of a durable national identity preserved across centuries of foreign rule. Medieval Bagratid Armenia centred on the city of Ani, and a separate Armenian kingdom flourished in Cilicia, while monasteries such as Haghpat, Sanahin and Geghard now stand as UNESCO World Heritage Sites. After partition among Persian, Ottoman and Russian empires, Armenians endured the genocide of 1915, a short-lived First Republic (1918), Soviet rule, and finally independence on 21 September 1991 — followed by a long and unresolved conflict with Azerbaijan over Nagorno-Karabakh.

founding.label: Independence from the Soviet Union
founding.detail: Modern Armenia dates its statehood to 21 September 1991, when an overwhelming majority of voters approved independence in a referendum and the Supreme Council declared the country's secession from the dissolving Soviet Union; the United States recognised Armenia's independence on 25 December 1991. But Armenian statehood reaches back millennia. The Iron Age kingdom of Urartu flourished in the Armenian highlands in the 9th and 8th centuries BCE, succeeded by the Armenians by the 6th century BCE; under Tigranes II 'the Great' (95–55 BCE) Armenia became briefly the strongest state in the Roman East. Around 301 CE Armenia became the first nation to adopt Christianity as its state religion, and the Armenian alphabet devised by Mesrop Mashtots around 405 anchored a literary culture that survived the loss of political independence for most of the next 1,500 years. A First Republic existed from 1918 to 1920 before Sovietisation, making 1991 the restoration of an interrupted statehood.

quickFacts.label: Political founding
quickFacts.value: Independence from the USSR declared 21 September 1991 (referendum)

quickFacts.label: Deep past
quickFacts.value: Urartu (9th–8th c. BCE); Armenian kingdom under Tigranes the Great, 95–55 BCE

quickFacts.label: First Christian state
quickFacts.value: Adopted Christianity as its religion c. 301 CE under Tiridates III

quickFacts.label: Armenian alphabet
quickFacts.value: Created c. 405 CE by Mesrop Mashtots (36 letters)

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Yerevan, capital since 1920; the fortress of Erebuni dates to 782 BCE

quickFacts.label: First Republic
quickFacts.value: Republic of Armenia 1918–1920, before Soviet rule (1920–1991)

eras.title: Urartu and the Armenian Kingdom
eras.standfirst: From the Iron Age kingdom of Urartu to the empire of Tigranes the Great, the Armenian highlands became a centre of power between Rome and Persia.
body: The Armenian highlands — the highest of the plateaus of West Asia, ringed by the Caucasus, the Anatolian plateau and the Iranian world — hold one of humanity's oldest records of settled life. In the early 1st millennium BCE the kingdom of Urartu, centred on Lake Van, rose to considerable power in the 9th and 8th centuries BCE, building fortress-cities and elaborate irrigation works. The fortress of Erebuni, on the site of modern Yerevan, was founded in 782 BCE. Mentioned in Assyrian sources, Urartu was succeeded in the region by the Armenians by the 6th century BCE.

Under the Orontid and then the Artaxiad dynasties, an Armenian kingdom emerged as a buffer and a prize between the great empires of the age. Its zenith came under Tigranes II, called 'the Great', who ruled from 95 to 55 BCE. Taking the ancient title 'king of kings', Tigranes expanded his realm until it briefly reached from the Caspian Sea toward the Mediterranean, making Armenia for a short time the strongest state in the Roman East. He founded a new royal city, Tigranocerta, before Roman armies under Lucullus and Pompey checked his ambitions.

After Tigranes, Armenia was repeatedly contested between Rome and Parthian (later Sasanian) Persia, often ruled by client kings of the Arsacid line and frequently partitioned between the two powers. This pattern — a distinct Armenian polity caught between larger empires — would recur for two thousand years. The Arsacid kingdom of Armenia endured until 428 CE, when the Sasanians abolished the monarchy and governed Armenia through appointed officials, ending the era of an independent ancient kingdom.

events.title: Founding of Erebuni
events.summary: The Urartian king Argishti I founded the fortress of Erebuni on the site of present-day Yerevan, one of the oldest continuously inhabited city sites in the world.

events.title: Urartu at its height
events.summary: In the 9th and 8th centuries BCE the kingdom of Urartu, centred on Lake Van, became a major power of the Middle East before being succeeded in the region by the Armenians.

events.title: Reign of Tigranes the Great
events.summary: Tigranes II expanded Armenia until it briefly stretched from the Caspian toward the Mediterranean, taking the title 'king of kings' and making Armenia the strongest state in the Roman East before Roman armies forced his retreat.

events.title: End of the Armenian monarchy
events.summary: The Sasanian Persians abolished the Arsacid kingdom of Armenia and ruled the territory through appointed officials, ending the ancient Armenian monarchy.

figures.name: Tigranes II 'the Great'
figures.blurb: Under Tigranes the Armenian kingdom reached its greatest extent, briefly stretching from the Caspian toward the Mediterranean; he took the title 'king of kings' and founded the city of Tigranocerta.

pullquote.text: Under Tigranes the Great, Armenia became for a short time the strongest state in the Roman East.

eras.title: The First Christian Nation and the Alphabet
eras.standfirst: Armenia became the first state to adopt Christianity, and the invention of its alphabet around 405 forged a literary identity that outlasted political independence.
body: Around 301 CE, King Tiridates III — converted, by tradition, after being healed by Gregory the Illuminator — proclaimed Christianity the religion of his kingdom, making Armenia the first nation to adopt Christianity as its state religion, a decade before Constantine's Edict of Milan. The Mother Cathedral of Echmiatsin, traditionally built in 301–303 by Tiridates and Gregory, became the spiritual centre of the Armenian Apostolic Church and remains its seat. Gregory the Illuminator was consecrated as the first head, or catholicos, of the Armenian Church.

A second foundation of national identity followed around 405, when the monk and scholar Mesrop Mashtots, with the support of the catholicos Sahak (Isaac) the Great, devised the Armenian alphabet of 36 letters. The new script allowed the Bible and liturgy to be rendered in Armenian for the first time, deepening the hold of Christianity and launching a golden age of translation and original writing in the 5th century. The alphabet, still in use today, became the durable vessel of Armenian culture through centuries without statehood.

Christianity and the written language sustained Armenian identity as the homeland was fought over by the Byzantine Empire and Sasanian Persia, and later overrun by the Arab caliphate from the 7th century. Through this long period of foreign domination, the Church and a distinctive architecture of domed stone churches — preserved at sites such as Echmiatsin and the ruins of Zvartnots, together inscribed by UNESCO in 2000 — kept a sense of nationhood alive even as political power passed to outside empires.

events.title: Armenia adopts Christianity
events.summary: King Tiridates III proclaimed Christianity the religion of his kingdom after his conversion by Gregory the Illuminator, making Armenia the first nation to adopt Christianity as its state religion.

events.title: Mother Cathedral of Echmiatsin
events.summary: The earliest of Armenia's great domed churches, the Mother Cathedral of Echmiatsin, was traditionally built by Tiridates III and Gregory the Illuminator; it remains the seat of the Armenian Apostolic Church.

events.title: Mesrop Mashtots creates the Armenian alphabet
events.summary: The scholar Mesrop Mashtots devised the 36-letter Armenian alphabet, enabling Scripture and literature in Armenian and launching a 5th-century golden age of writing and translation.

figures.name: Tiridates III
figures.role: King of Armenia; converted to Christianity c. 301
figures.blurb: After his conversion by Gregory the Illuminator, Tiridates made Christianity the religion of his kingdom around 301, making Armenia the world's first officially Christian state.

figures.name: Mesrop Mashtots
figures.role: Monk, scholar and creator of the Armenian alphabet
figures.blurb: Devised the Armenian alphabet around 405, allowing the Bible and liturgy to be written in Armenian and anchoring a literary culture that long outlived political independence.

pullquote.text: The alphabet permitted ordinary people to read the Bible for the first time, helping to entrench Christianity in Armenia.

eras.title: Bagratid Ani and Cilician Armenia
eras.standfirst: A revived kingdom flowered at Ani, the 'city of a thousand churches', while a second Armenian kingdom rose far to the south in Cilicia.
body: Armenian statehood revived in 885, when Ashot I of the Bagratuni (Bagratid) family was recognised as king by both the Abbasid caliph and the Byzantine emperor. Under the Bagratids the city of Ani, made the capital by Ashot III around 961, became a great medieval centre, renowned as the 'city of a thousand churches' and praised for cathedral and palace architecture that ranks among the finest of the Armenian Middle Ages. In this era the monastic complexes of Haghpat and Sanahin in northern Armenia became leading centres of learning, their churches representing the highest flowering of Armenian religious architecture.

The Bagratid kingdom fell to Byzantine annexation in 1045 and then to the Seljuk Turks, whose advance after the Battle of Manzikert (1071) pushed many Armenians westward. Ani was sacked, struck by a devastating earthquake in 1319, and slid into ruin as trade routes shifted. The political centre of Armenian life moved south, away from the highland heartland that would remain under Turkic and Persian rule for centuries.

On the Mediterranean coast, Armenian refugees and princes built a new realm in Cilicia. Founded as a principality around 1080 under the Rubenid dynasty, it became the Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia when Leo II was crowned king Leo I on 6 January 1198. Allied at times with the Crusader states and a hub of Mediterranean trade, Cilician Armenia endured nearly three centuries until it fell to the Mamluks of Egypt in 1375, ending the last medieval Armenian kingdom.

events.title: Bagratid kingdom restored
events.summary: Ashot I of the Bagratuni dynasty was recognised as king of Armenia by both the caliph and the Byzantine emperor, reviving an independent Armenian monarchy.

events.title: Ani becomes the Bagratid capital
events.summary: King Ashot III moved the capital to Ani, which flourished as a major medieval city known as the 'city of a thousand churches' for its cathedral and monastic architecture.

events.title: Kingdom of Cilician Armenia
events.summary: Leo II was crowned king Leo I, raising the Armenian principality in Cilicia to a kingdom that, allied with the Crusaders, lasted until its conquest by the Mamluks in 1375.

events.title: Fall of Cilician Armenia
events.summary: The Mamluks of Egypt conquered the Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia, extinguishing the last independent medieval Armenian state.

figures.name: Ashot I the Great
figures.blurb: Founder of the Bagratid kingdom, recognised as king by both the Abbasid caliph and the Byzantine emperor, restoring Armenian statehood after centuries of foreign rule.

pullquote.text: Ani, the Bagratid capital, was renowned as the 'city of a thousand churches', a high point of medieval Armenian architecture.
pullquote.attribution: On the golden age of Ani

eras.title: Empires, the Genocide and the First Republic
eras.standfirst: Partitioned among Persia, the Ottomans and Russia, the Armenians of the empire suffered the genocide of 1915 — and then, briefly, won an independent republic.
body: After the fall of Cilicia, the Armenian homeland was divided among rival empires. By the 16th and 17th centuries it lay split between Ottoman Turkey in the west and Safavid Persia in the east, with populations repeatedly uprooted by war. From 1813 and especially 1828, Russia annexed eastern (Persian) Armenia, so that by the 19th century Armenians lived as subject communities under three imperial regimes — Ottoman, Russian and, by then residually, Persian — without a state of their own.

Within the Ottoman Empire, Armenian demands for reform and rising nationalism met with violent repression, culminating in catastrophe during the First World War. Beginning on 24 April 1915 with the arrest of Armenian leaders and intellectuals in Constantinople, the ruling Young Turk government (the Committee of Union and Progress) carried out the systematic deportation and mass killing of the empire's Armenians, driving them on death marches toward the Syrian desert amid massacre, starvation and exposure. The Armenian Genocide is widely recognised as the first genocide of the 20th century; estimates of the dead generally range from around 600,000 to as many as 1.5 million, and 24 April is commemorated by Armenians worldwide.

Out of the empires' wartime collapse came a brief independence. As the Russian Empire disintegrated, the Armenians of the Caucasus declared the Republic of Armenia on 28 May 1918, the First Republic, with its capital at Yerevan. Beset by war, refugees, famine and conflict with neighbours, the fragile state survived only two years. In late 1920 it was overwhelmed between advancing Turkish nationalist forces and the Soviet Red Army, and on 2 December 1920 it was Sovietised, ending the First Republic.

events.title: Russia annexes eastern Armenia
events.summary: Following war with Persia, Russia annexed the eastern (Persian) Armenian lands, dividing the Armenian homeland between the Russian and Ottoman empires.

events.title: The Armenian Genocide
events.summary: From 24 April 1915 the Ottoman Young Turk government deported and massacred the empire's Armenian population in death marches toward the Syrian desert; widely recognised as the first genocide of the 20th century, with deaths estimated from roughly 600,000 to as many as 1.5 million.

events.title: First Republic of Armenia
events.summary: As the Russian Empire collapsed, Armenians of the Caucasus declared an independent Republic of Armenia with its capital at Yerevan, the first modern Armenian state.

events.title: Sovietisation of Armenia
events.summary: Squeezed between Turkish nationalist forces and the advancing Red Army, the First Republic fell and Armenia was Sovietised, ending its short-lived independence.

figures.name: Gregory the Illuminator
figures.role: Patron saint and first head of the Armenian Church
figures.blurb: Credited with converting King Tiridates III and establishing Christianity in Armenia around 301; his cathedral at Echmiatsin remains the centre of the Armenian Apostolic Church.

pullquote.text: At least 664,000 and possibly as many as 1.2 million Armenians died during the genocide of 1915–16.

eras.title: Soviet Armenia
eras.standfirst: For seven decades Armenia was a Soviet republic — industrialised, urbanised and rebuilt after disaster, yet always keeping its Church, language and memory of the genocide.
body: In March 1922 Soviet Armenia was joined with Georgia and Azerbaijan in the Transcaucasian Soviet Federated Socialist Republic, which became part of the U.S.S.R. that December. In 1936, under Stalin, the federation was dissolved and Armenia became a separate union republic, the Armenian Soviet Socialist Republic. Under Soviet rule the largely rural country was rapidly industrialised and urbanised; Yerevan grew into a modern capital, and a diaspora scattered by the genocide maintained ties to the homeland.

Soviet Armenians lived under the constraints of one-party rule and periodic repression, yet the Armenian Apostolic Church survived and national memory endured. In 1965, on the fiftieth anniversary of the genocide, mass demonstrations in Yerevan pressed for its commemoration, and a memorial, Tsitsernakaberd, was built on a hill above the city. The republic also suffered natural catastrophe: in December 1988 a powerful earthquake around Spitak and Leninakan (Gyumri) killed tens of thousands and left hundreds of thousands homeless.

As Soviet control loosened under Mikhail Gorbachev's reforms, long-suppressed tensions surfaced. In 1988 the regional council of Nagorno-Karabakh, an Armenian-majority enclave within Soviet Azerbaijan, voted to join Armenia, igniting a dispute that would harden into war. Amid the collapse of the Soviet Union, Armenia moved swiftly toward independence, holding a referendum in September 1991 in which voters chose to secede.

events.title: Armenian SSR established
events.summary: The Transcaucasian federation was dissolved and Armenia became a separate union republic of the U.S.S.R., the Armenian Soviet Socialist Republic.

events.title: Nagorno-Karabakh dispute erupts
events.summary: The regional legislature of Nagorno-Karabakh, an Armenian-majority enclave within Soviet Azerbaijan, voted to join Armenia, triggering a conflict that escalated into open war.

events.title: Spitak earthquake
events.summary: A catastrophic earthquake struck northern Armenia around Spitak and Gyumri, killing tens of thousands and leaving hundreds of thousands homeless in the final Soviet years.

eras.title: Independent Armenia
eras.standfirst: Independence in 1991 reopened Armenian statehood — and a long, unresolved conflict with Azerbaijan over Nagorno-Karabakh that reshaped the region into the 2020s.
body: On 21 September 1991 Armenians voted overwhelmingly for independence, and the Supreme Council declared secession from the U.S.S.R.; Levon Ter-Petrosyan became the new republic's first president, and the United States extended recognition on 25 December 1991. The young state faced an immediate war: the dispute over Nagorno-Karabakh exploded into full-scale fighting after 1991, leaving roughly 30,000 dead and creating hundreds of thousands of refugees before a Russian-brokered ceasefire in 1994 left Armenian forces in control of the enclave and surrounding districts.

The frozen conflict reshaped Armenia's politics and economy, which struggled with blockade by Turkey and Azerbaijan even as the country built democratic institutions. In 2018 a peaceful mass protest movement, the 'Velvet Revolution', brought down the entrenched governing elite and made the journalist-turned-politician Nikol Pashinyan prime minister. The Nagorno-Karabakh question, treated very differently by the two sides, remained the defining issue of national life.

In the autumn of 2020 a 44-day war saw Azerbaijan, with Turkish and Israeli weaponry, retake much of the territory around Nagorno-Karabakh; a Russian-brokered ceasefire on 9 November 2020 ended the fighting. In September 2023 an Azerbaijani offensive brought the enclave fully under Azerbaijani control, and within roughly a week well over 100,000 ethnic Armenians fled to Armenia. The self-declared Nagorno-Karabakh authorities were dissolved at the start of 2024. By 2025 Armenia and Azerbaijan were negotiating toward a peace settlement, as a small, landlocked but ancient nation worked to define its future after the loss of Karabakh.

events.title: Independence from the Soviet Union
events.summary: Armenians approved independence by referendum and the Supreme Council declared secession from the U.S.S.R.; the United States recognised Armenia on 25 December 1991.

events.title: First Nagorno-Karabakh War ceasefire
events.summary: After fighting that left roughly 30,000 dead (estimate) and hundreds of thousands displaced, a 1994 ceasefire left Armenian forces in de facto control of Nagorno-Karabakh and adjacent districts.

events.title: The Velvet Revolution
events.summary: A peaceful protest movement led by Nikol Pashinyan brought down the governing elite and installed a new reform government, with Pashinyan elected prime minister.

events.title: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War
events.summary: In a 44-day war Azerbaijan retook much of the territory around Nagorno-Karabakh; a Russian-brokered ceasefire on 9 November 2020 ended the fighting, with heavy casualties on both sides (estimates).

events.title: Azerbaijani offensive and the Karabakh exodus
events.summary: An Azerbaijani offensive brought Nagorno-Karabakh fully under its control; within about a week well over 100,000 ethnic Armenians fled to Armenia, and the enclave's authorities were dissolved by 1 January 2024.

figures.name: Levon Ter-Petrosyan
figures.blurb: Led Armenia to independence in 1991 and through the first years of statehood and the first Nagorno-Karabakh War before resigning in 1998.

pullquote.text: Within about a week in September 2023, well over 100,000 ethnic Armenians left Nagorno-Karabakh for Armenia.

figures.name: Tigranes II 'the Great'
figures.blurb: Ruled the ancient Armenian kingdom at its greatest extent, briefly the strongest state in the Roman East, before Roman armies under Lucullus and Pompey forced his retreat.

figures.name: Tiridates III
figures.blurb: Converted by Gregory the Illuminator, he proclaimed Christianity the religion of his kingdom around 301, making Armenia the world's first officially Christian state.

figures.name: Mesrop Mashtots
figures.blurb: Devised the 36-letter Armenian alphabet around 405, enabling a written Armenian Bible and literature and anchoring a culture that survived centuries without a state.
